coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots as a nitric oxide donor can promote wound healing of deep partial-thickness burns in rats
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Tang, Tao
Liu, Yingying
Wang, Peng
Xiang, Yang
Liu, Lei
Xiao, Shichu
Wang, Guangyi
coredata.dc:description:
Introduction In this study, a new carbon quantum dots-NO (CQDs-NO) that is based on spermidine trihydrochloride and can be used as a nitric oxide donor was prepared using a two-step hyperthermia-intermittent ultrasonic method, after which its healing effect on deep partial-thickness burn wounds was tested in rats.
Materials and Methods CQDs-NO were prepared by a two-step hyperthermia-intermittent ultrasonic method. NO-released rate and biocompatibility of CQDs-NO were tested. The biological functions of CQDs-NO were measured by scratch assay, Western blotting, histology, and transcriptome sequencing.
Results CQDs-NO with a concentration of 1 μg/mL and 5 μg/mL showed no cytotoxicity. CQDs-NO could release NO when co-cultured with cells or glutathione peroxidase. We also found that CQDs-NO promotes the biological processes such as angiogenesis, cell-substrate adhesion, extracellular matrix organization, cell migration, and wound healing in human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVEC). Additionally, CQDs-NO promoted wound healing of deep partial-thickness burn by enhancing vascularization, matrix deposition, as well as regulating the inflammatory reactions of wounds.
Conclusions CQDs-NO could be used as an alternative method for deep partial-thickness burn healing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Deep partial-thickness burn wounds
Nitric oxide

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and characterization of carbon quantum dots-NO (CQDs-NO)
In vivo test of CQDS-NO on deep partial-thickness burn wound healing
CQDs-NO can release NO continuously when co-cultured with HUVEC, and GPX can induce CQDS-NO to release NO
CQDs-NO can promote HUVEC migration by releasing NO
CQDs-NO can increase the expression of TGFβ2, HBEGF, and LAMB1 protein by releasing NO
CQDs-NO promotes HUVEC to participate in biological processes such as angiogenesis, cell-substrate adhesion, extracellular matrix organization, cell migration, and wound healing
CQDs promotes the vascularization and wound healing of deep partial-thickness burns

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) CQDs prepared by two-step hyperthermia-intermittent ultrasonic method, TEM shows the particle size of 9.9 ± 0.9 nm. (B) The color of CQDs under white light (left) and ultraviolet light (right). (C) FTIR spectra of CQDs-NO. (D) MS of CQDs-NO.

(A) CCK-8 assay suggested that 1, 5 ug/ml CQDs-NO had no significant cytotoxicity vs. control cells (p > 0.05), while 25 ug/ml CQDs-NO (p<0.05) has certain cytotoxicity. (B) Live/Dead assay suggested that 1, 5 ug/ml CQDs-NO had no significant cytotoxicity, while 25 ug/ml CQDs-NO had certain cytotoxicity.

NO release in vitro. During cell culture, a small amount of NO was continuously produced by self-metabolism, and the total released NO in 24 h was 0.9751 ± 0.2751 uM; 1, 5 ug/ml CQDs-NO released 0.01391 ± 0.04231 uM and 0.1530 ± 0.1396 uM NO in 24 h without cells. Under the action of glutathione peroxidase, 1, 5 ug/ml CQDs-NO released 0.5205 ± 0.05493 uM and 1.180 ± 0.08514 uM NO in 24 h, respectively. Co-cultured with HUVEC cells, 1, 5 ug/ml CQDs-NO continuously released NO within 24 h and released 2.042 ± 0.1090 uM and 3.523 ± 0.3500 uM NO within 24 h in total.

(A) The scratch test shows that 1, 5 ug/ml CQDs-NO can effectively promote the migration of vascular endothelial cells, while NO antagonist carboxy-PITO inhibits the effect of CQDs-NO on promoting the migration of vascular endothelial cells. (B) Changes in scrath test. (C) Western blot indicated that 1, 5ug/ml CQDs-NO could promote the expression of TGFβ2, HBEGF, and LAMB1 protein in HUVEC, while NO antagonist carboxy-PITO inhibited the effect of CQDs-NO on the expression of TGFβ2, HBEGF, and LAMB1 protein.

(A) mRNA differentially expressed after of HUVEC full transcriptome sequencing, ctrl: 0 ug/ml CQDs-NO, CQDl: 1 ug/ml CQDs-NO, CQDh: 5 ug/ml CQDs-NO. (B) GO enrichment analysis indicated that 1 ug/ml and 5 ug/ml CQDs-NO could promote the increase of mRNA expression of genes related to the biological process of angiogenesis, such as ITGAV, STAB1, MCAM, TGFB2, EPAS1, THBS1, LOXL2, PLCD3, FN1, ERBB2, PLXND1, HSPG2, MYH9, GATA2, DAG1, COL18A1, AMOTL2, ITGB8, MMP2, CARD10, DAB2IP, WARS1, HMOX1, CCN1, SPARC, TGFBI, COL8A1. (C) GO enrichment analysis showed that 1 ug/ml and 5 ug/ml CQDs-NO could promote the increase of mRNA expression of genes involved in the biological process of cell-substrate adhesion, such as CD44, PTPRJ, CCDC80, CCN1, PRKCE, VCL, COL8A1, ADAM15, ITGAV, FN1, FLNA, THBS1, CALR, SMAD6, NID1, LAMB1, ITGA3, ROCK1, ANTXR1, BCAM, DAG1, SMAD3, FGG. (D) GO enrichment analysis showed that 1 ug/ml and 5 ug/ml CQDs-NO could promote the increase of mRNA expression of genes related to the biological process of extracellular matrix organization, such as SERPINH1, FLRT2, BMP1, CCDC80, LOXL1, CCN1, SPARC, TGFBI, COL8A1, DAG1, LAMB2, COL18A1, HSPG2, ANTXR1, ITGB8, BSG, NID1, TIMP2, LAMB1, ITGA3, PLOD3, THBS1, LOXL2, COL5A1, FN1, ERCC2, CD44, LOX, TGFB2, MMP2, AGRN, COL5A2, FGG, COL9A3, COL6A1, FBN1, ITGAV, COL4A5. (E) GO enrichment analysis indicated that 1 ug/ml and 5 ug/ml CQDs-NO could promote the increase of mRNA expression of genes related to biological process of positive regulation of cell migration ITGA3, KITLG, EMA3A, SEMA3C, MCAM, SSH1, LAMB1, TGFB2, HMOX1, AKT2 HBEGF, SPARC, FN1, TNFSF18, DAB2IP, THBS1, KIF20B, ITGAV, TP53, CCN1, SEMA3D, ATOH8, PRKCE, CALR, COL18A1, FLNA. (F) GO enrichment analysis indicated that 1 ug/ml and 5 ug/ml CQDs-NO could promote the increase of mRNA expression of genes related to the biological process of wound healing, such as EHD2, CD44, VCL, MCAM, TGFB2, P2RX6, HMOX1, MYH9, ITPK1, ENO3, HBEGF, LOX, SPARC, FN1, COL5A1, TLR4, ARRB1, THBS1, TPM1, TP53, ERBB2, CCN1, GSN, SLC7A11, PRKCE, FGG, DAG1, GATA2, PRKAR1B, FLNA, PAPSS2.

(A) CQDs-NO could significantly accelerate deep partial-thickness burns wound healing. Control: deep partial-thickness burn; CQDs-NO: 1 ml 200 ug/ml CQDs-NO was applied to deep partial-thickness burns. (B) Compared with the control group, CQDs-NO significantly promoted the epithelization of deep partial-thickness burn wounds. (C) Compared with the control group, CQDs-NO significantly promoted the deposition of the dermal matrix in deep partial-thickness burn wounds.

(A) CQDs-NO significantly promoted the vascularization of burn wounds, and the number of CD31+ cells increased significantly. (B) CQDs-NO significantly inhibited the inflammation of deep partial-thickness burn wound, shown as the number of CD68+ cells significantly decreased. (C) CQDs-NO significantly promoted the expression of Lamb1 in deep partial-thickness burn wounds.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots as a nitric oxide donor can promote wound healing of deep partial-thickness burns in rats

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bDepartment of Burns and Plastic Surgery Linfen Central Hospital, Linfen, Shanxi, 041000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Plastic Surgery, the First Affiliated Hospital of Naval Medical University, Shanghai 200433, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: the First Affiliated Hospital of Naval Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cDepartment of Plastic Surgery, the First Affiliated Hospital of Naval Medical University

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contribute equally to this article.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a new carbon quantum dots-NO (CQDs-NO) that is based on spermidine trihydrochloride and can be used as a nitric oxide donor was prepared using a two-step hyperthermia-intermittent ultrasonic method, after which its healing effect on deep partial-thickness burn wounds was tested in rats.

CQDs-NO were prepared by a two-step hyperthermia-intermittent ultrasonic method. NO-released rate and biocompatibility of CQDs-NO were tested. The biological functions of CQDs-NO were measured by scratch assay, Western blotting, histology, and transcriptome sequencing.

CQDs-NO with a concentration of 1 μg/mL and 5 μg/mL showed no cytotoxicity. CQDs-NO could release NO when co-cultured with cells or glutathione peroxidase. We also found that CQDs-NO promotes the biological processes such as angiogenesis, cell-substrate adhesion, extracellular matrix organization, cell migration, and wound healing in human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVEC). Additionally, CQDs-NO promoted wound healing of deep partial-thickness burn by enhancing vascularization, matrix deposition, as well as regulating the inflammatory reactions of wounds.
CQDs-NO could be used as an alternative method for deep partial-thickness burn healing.

ce:list-item.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Wound healing has been one of the hottest topics in clinical and basic research (Partridge et al., 2018). Deep partial-thickness burns involve the repair of the dermis, which leads to scar healing. Therefore, preventing infection, regulating inflammatory reactions of the wounds, and promoting vascularization and re-epithelialization are of great clinical significance for accelerating wound healing of deep partial-thickness burns, reducing scar hyperplasia, and improving patients’ quality of life.

Over recent years, carbon dot material with good biocompatibility has attracted much attention in the medical field. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is a relatively new carbon material whose application was first reported in 2004 (Xu et al., 2004). In a broad sense, CDs refer to fluorescent carbon particles < 20 nm in size (Xu et al., 2004). Its chemical structure is characterized by carbon featuring sp2-sp3 orbital hybridization, which mainly includes graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with obvious lattice and carbon nanodots (CNDs) with inconspicuous lattice (Lim et al., 2015). The fluorescent probe prepared based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) can be used for visual detection of tissues and localization and movement of intracellular molecules such as DNA and RNA (Gong et al., 2015; Yang et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2020). Also, CQDs with a specific structure can induce the apoptosis of tumor cells through the specific action of oxygen free radicals or surface modification groups, thus achieving an anti-tumor effect (Saini and Kim, 2019). Furthermore, most CQDs have the characteristic of photoluminescence due to protonation and deprotonation of surface groups or specific lattice structures (Gong et al., 2015; Yuan et al., 2015). Besides, CQDs with specific structures or specific surface groups can achieve antibacterial effects through photodynamic effects, peroxidase-like effects, mechanical/physical damage, inhibition of microbial metabolism, etc. (Mei et al., 2020; Nie et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2014; Liang et al., 2020). Given the diversity of CQDs structures, a series of surface functionalization can be carried out, such as surface modification by hydrogen donor groups or anti-oxidation by mixing antioxidative atomic groups (Zhao et al., 2015; Das et al., 2019; Gao et al., 2020).

Over the years, CQDs have been applied for studying chronic wounds and infectious wounds (Mei et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2014; Liang et al., 2020; Bankoti et al., 2017). However, their effect and mechanism on key cells related to wound healing, such as vascular endothelial cells, epidermal cells, and fibroblasts, are still not fully understood. It has been found that specific CQDs can promote the cell migration of fibroblasts (Abolghasemzade et al., 2021). Meanwhile, the effect of CQDs on burn wound healing and its influence on key factors such as vascularization, epithelialization, and matrix deposition in the process of burn wound healing have not been reported so far.

Nitric oxide can effectively regulate biological processes such as vascularization, cell proliferation, cell migration, and inflammatory reaction (Malone-Povolny et al., 2019). NONOate, as a nitric oxide donor, has been used to promote the scientific research and clinical treatment of vascularization (such as Spermine NONOate and sodium nitroprusside). Also, NONOate based on spermine/spermidine is optimal for promoting the vascularization effect (Majumder et al., 2014). In this study, carbon quantum dots-NO (CQDs-NO) based on spermidine trihydrochloride, which can be used as a nitric oxide donor, was prepared using a two-step hyperthermia-intermittent ultrasonic method and then applied to deep partial-thickness burn wounds in rats. It was found that CQDS-NO could promote the vascularization and epithelialization of the wounds and regulate the inflammation of wounds through the sustained release of NO, thus promoting the healing of deep partial-thickness burn wounds.

Six-week-old male SD rats, weighing 200–210 g, were purchased from the Laboratory Animal Center of Naval Military Medical University (Shanghai, China). All the animals were housed in an environment with a temperature of 22 ± 1 °C, relative humidity of 50 ± 1%, and a light/dark cycle of 12/12 hr. All animal studies (including the rats euthanasia procedure) were done in compliance with the regulations and guidelines of First Affiliated Hospital of Naval Military Medical University institutional animal care and conducted according to the AAALAC and the IACUC guidelines.
Unless otherwise specified, all reagents were purchased from Sigma Aldrich (St. Louis, Missouri, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of carbon quantum dots-NO (CQDs-NO)
ce:section.ce:para: A 100 mg spermidine trihydrochloride was calcined at 275 °C for three hours. After dissolving the product in 5 ml ddH2O, the suspension liquid was dispersed by an intermittent ultrasound procedure in ice at 500 W, 60 s × 45 times, and then centrifugated with 20,000 g for 60 min at 4 °C. The supernatant was dialysis for six times with a 1000 Da dialysis bag (YA1035, Solarbio, Shanghai, China), for 4 h each time. A particle analyzer (Mastersizer 2000; Malvern Instruments, Malvern, UK) was used to measure the zeta potential of CQDs-NO, and the form of CQDs-NO was observed by transmission electron microscope (Tecnai G2spirit Biotwin, 120 kV). X-rmy diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, and Mass Spectrometry was used to detect the crystal structure and possible surface groups of CQDS-NO.

Human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVEC) were purchased from Procell Biology (Wuhan, China) and cultured in DMEM supplemented with 10% (v/v) FBS and 1% (v/v) penicillin-streptomycin in a humidified atmosphere containing 5%CO2 at 37 °C. The culture media of the cells were changed once per 2d

Approximately 1 × 105 HUVEC cells were plated in a 24 well plate diameter dish and cultured overnight. Then, 1 μg/mL CQDs-NO, 5 μg/mL CQDs-NO, 1 μg/mL CQDs-NO + 10 μg/mL glutathione peroxidase (Gpx), and 5 μg/mL CQDs-NO + 10 μg/mL glutathione peroxidase (n = 3) were added into the 24-well plate without cells. The DMEM complete culture medium with 0, 1, 5 μg/mL CQDs and 10% FBS was added to 24-well plates for HUVEC culture. The supernatant was collected at 0/3/6/12/24 h after adding CQDs-NO and analyzed using nitric oxide testing Kit (S0021S, Byotime, Guangzhou, China) according to the manufacturer's instructions to determine the concentration of nitric oxide.

HUVEC was cultured in the DMEM medium containing 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) in 96-well plates (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA). Cells were inoculated at a density of 5 × 104 cells/well and cultured for 24 h before adding 0 μg/mL (Control), 1 μg/mL, and 25 μg/mL CQDS-NO. Consequently, 100 μL of sterile CCK-8 dye was added to each well and incubated for another 2 h at 37 °C, following the CCK-8 Cell Proliferation and Cytotoxicity Assay Kit (ab228554, abcam, USA). A Live/Dead Cell Double Staining Kit (ab115347, abcam, USA) was used according to the manufacturer's instructions to determine viable and dead cells.

A Scratch test was used to determine the migration ability of cells. Briefly, 4 × 105 HUVEC cells were cultured in a 6-well plate overnight. After being washed with PBS, a line was drawn using a marker on the bottom of the dish, and then a sterile 200-μL pipet tip was used to scratch three separate wounds through the cells. The cells were gently rinsed twice with PBS to remove floating cells and incubated in the DMEM medium with 1% FBS added with 0 μg/mL CQDs-NO (Control), 50 μg/mL Carboxy-PITO (Byotime, Guangzhou, China), 1 μg/mL CQDs-NO, 1 μg/mL CQDs-NO + 50 μg/mL Carboxy-PITO, 5 μg/mL CQDs-NO, 5 μg/mL CQDs-NO + 50 μg/mL Carboxy-PITO for 24 h. Images of the scratches were taken using an inverted microscope at 100 × magnification at 0 and 24 h of incubation.

1 × 107 HUVEC cells were cultured overnight in a 10 cm culture plate. After being washed with PBS, these cells were cultured in DMEM medium with 10% FBS containing 0 μg/mL CQDs-NO (Control), 50 μg/mL Carboxy-PITO (Byotime, Guangzhou, China), 1 μg/mL CQDs-NO, 1 μg/mL CQDs-NO + 50 μg/mL Carboxy-PITO, 5 μg/mL CQDs-NO, 5 μg/mL CQDs-NO + 50 μg/mL Carboxy-PITO for 24 h. After extracting the total cellular protein by RIPA Lysis buffer, proteins were loaded on a 8% and 10% SDS-PAGE (polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis), then transferred to 0.2 μm polyvinylidene difluoride (PVDF) membrane, blocked and incubated with TGFβ2(sc-374658, santa cruz, USA), HBEGF (ab66792, abcam, USA) and Lamininβ1 (LAMB1) antibodies (ab44941, abcam, USA) at 4 °C overnight. ECL chemiluminescent reagent (Thermoscientic, Rockford, IL, USA) showed immunoreactive protein bands. The gel recording system was used for density measurement.

HUVEC cells were cultured in 6-well plates in a DMEM medium supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum. Cells were plated at a density of 107 cells/well, cultured for 24 h, and then incubated with 0 μg/mL (Control), 1 μg/mL, or 5 μg/mL CQDs-NO for an additional 24 h. Total RNA was extracted using trizol. A total amount of 3 μg RNA per sample was then used as input material for the RNA sample preparations. Sequencing libraries were generated using NEBNext® UltraTM RNA Library Prep Kit for Illumina® (NEB, USA) following manufacturer's recommendations. The index codes were added to attribute sequences to each sample. Library quality was assessed on the Agilent Bioanalyzer 2100 system. The clustering of the index-coded samples was performed on a cBot ClusterGeneration System using TruSeq PE Cluster Kit v3-cBot-HS (Illumia) following the manufacturer's protocol. After cluster generation, the library preparations were sequenced on an Illumina Hiseq platform, and 125 bp/150 bp paired-end reads were generated. Gene Ontology (GO) enrichment analysis of differentially expressed genes was implemented by the clusterProfiler R package, in which gene length bias was corrected. We used clusterProfiler R package to test the statistical enrichment of differential expression genes in KEGG pathways. GO terms with corrected and P-value < 0.05 were considered significantly enriched by differential expressed genes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo test of CQDS-NO on deep partial-thickness burn wound healing
ce:section.ce:para: Preparation of deep partial-thickness burn model: 10 SD rats were anesthetized after inhaling isoflurane, and their back hair was removed. The copper block that was boiled in 100 °C hot water for 5 min was then placed on the back skin of the rats for 10 s. The wounds were covered with vaseline gauze and sterile gauze.

After the rats were injured, those in the control group were sprayed with 0.5 ml ddH2O, while the experimental group was sprayed with 0.5 ml 400 μg/mL CQDs-NO. The external dressing was changed every day. The wound healing was recorded at 0, 5, 10, 15, and 20 days after the injury. On the 20th day after the injury, the full-thickness skin of the back was taken, fixed with paraformaldehyde, and embedded in paraffin. The HE/Masson staining and immunohistochemical staining with CD31 (ab182981, abcam, USA)/CD68 (MA5–13324, Invitrogen, USA)/Laminin β1 LAMB1(ab44941, abcam, USA) antibodies were applied to the slices.

All data analysis was performed with the SPSS17.0 statistical analysis software. Overall comparisons of grouped data were analyzed with a one-way analysis of variance. In addition, the unpaired Student's t-test was used to compare individual groups. Values are expressed as mean ± SD. A P value <0.05 was considered to be statistically significant.

The CQDs-NO prepared by the two-step hyperthermia-intermittent ultrasonic method has a particle size of 9.9 ± 0.9 nm (Fig. 1 A) and a zeta potential of 45.37 ± 3.73 mV. CQDs-NO shows remarkable photoluminescence (Fig. 1B) under ultraviolet excitation. FTIR and MS were carried out to analyze the surface chemistry of CQDS-NO (Fig. 1C). The possible surface groups of CQDs-NO are seen in Fig. 1C. Among them, the peak values of 3300 cm-1 to 3500 cm-1 may be the stretching vibration of N - H, the peak values of 2929 cm−1 may be the stretching vibration of CH2, and the peak values of 1615 cm−1, 1504 cm−1 and 1384 cm−1 may be the stretching vibration of NO of CNO. The wavelength of 1457 cm-1 may be the single bond vibration and fingerprint region of CH (Fig. 1C). Fig. 1D shows that the molecular formula corresponding to the peak value of m/z = 158 may be C7H17N3O. FITR and MS results suggest that there may be C N = O groups on the surface of CQDS-NO. XRD shows that the peak of CQDs-NO is at (Fig. S1) 2Ɵ ≈ 21.2, which confirms its amorphous lattice structure.

CCK-8 assay suggested that 1, 5 μg/mL of CQDs-NO had no significant cytotoxicity; no statistical difference was found compared to control cells (p > 0.05). Contrary, 25 μg/mL of CQDs-NO inhibited cell proliferation (Fig. 2 A). Moreover, the live/dead assay showed similar results (Fig. 2B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: CQDs-NO can release NO continuously when co-cultured with HUVEC, and GPX can induce CQDS-NO to release NO
ce:section.ce:para: The results of NO detection indicated that during HUVEC culture, the metabolism of HUVEC continuously produced NO. The total released NO in 24 h was 0.9751 ± 0.2751 uM; 1, 5 μg/mL CQDs-NO released 0.01391 ± 0.04231 uM and 0.1530 ± 0.1396 uM NO in 24 h without HUVEC. HUVEC started to release NO in the early stage under the action of glutathione peroxidase; 0.1064 ± 0.02575 uM and 0.4737 ± 0.03506 uM NO were released at 1 h; the total released NO in 24 h was 0.5205 ± 0.05493uM and 1.180 ± 0.08514uM, respectively. Co-cultured with HUVEC, 1, 5 μg/mL CQDs-NO continuously released NO within 24 h, and accumulated 2.042 ± 0.1090 uM and 3.523 ± 0.3500 uM NO within 24 h (Fig. 3 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: CQDs-NO can promote HUVEC migration by releasing NO
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the effect of CQDs-NO on HUVEC migration, we carried out scratch tests. The results showed that 1, 5 μg/mL CQDs-NO significantly promoted HUVEC migration, while Carboxy-PITO, as NO blocking agent, could inhibit this effect to some extent (Fig. 4 A).The scrath tests showed that the wound closure of control group was significantly fast than Carboxy-PITO group (86.66 ± 1.99% vs 75.08 ± 2.05%, *:p = 0.0153,n = 3), the wound closure of 1 μg/mL CQDs-NO group was significantly fast than 1 μg/mL CQDs-NO +Carboxy-PITO group (100.30±1.63% vs 64.69±2.11%,**:p = 0.0002,n = 3), the wound closure of 5 μg/mL CQDs-NO group was significantly fast than 5 μg/mL CQDs-NO +Carboxy-PITO group (100.0 ± 2.57% vs 64.30 ± 2.90%, ***:p = 0.0008,n = 3), the wound closure of 1 μg/mL CQDs-NO group was significantly fast than control group (100.30±1.63% vs 86.66 ± 1.99%, ****:p = 0.0061,n = 3), the wound closure of 5 μg/mL CQDs-NO group was significantly fast than control group (100.0 ± 2.57% vs 86.66 ± 1.99%, *****:p = 0.0146,n = 3) (Fig. 4B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: CQDs-NO can increase the expression of TGFβ2, HBEGF, and LAMB1 protein by releasing NO
ce:section.ce:para: Next, we analyzed the protein expression of TGF-β2, HBEGF, and LAMB1 in HUVEC stimulated by CQDs-NO by Western blot. The results showed that 1/5 μg/mL CQDs-NO could significantly promote the expression of TGF-β2, HBEGF, and LAMB1 in vascular endothelial cells, while Carboxy-PITO could inhibit the promotion of these proteins induced by CQDs-NO (Fig. 4C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: CQDs-NO promotes HUVEC to participate in biological processes such as angiogenesis, cell-substrate adhesion, extracellular matrix organization, cell migration, and wound healing
ce:section.ce:para: To study the biological effects of CQDs -NO on HUVEC, we sequenced the transcriptome of HUVEC stimulated by 0 (Control), 1, 5 μg/mL CQDs -NO (Fig. 5 A). GO enrichment suggests that CQDs -NO can significantly promote the biological processes of HUVEC, such as cell proliferation, cell migration, extracellular matrix deposition, vascularization, wound healing, and cell adhesion. GO enrichment showed that 1 μg/mL and 5 μg/mL CQDs-NO stimulation could promote the mRNA expression of HUVECs involved in biological processes such as angiogenesis, cell-substrate adhesion, extracellular matrix organization, cell migration, and wound healing (Fig. 5, Table S1, and Table S2). TGFβ2 was involved in most of these biological processes. Meanwhile, we noticed that the gene expression of HBEGF and LAMB1 was also significantly increased in HUVEC stimulated by 1, 5 μg/mL CQDs-NO compared to control cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CQDs promotes the vascularization and wound healing of deep partial-thickness burns
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the effect of CQDs-NO on deep partial-thickness burn wounds, we applied CQDs-NO to the animal models with deep partial-thickness burns and found that CQDs-NO could effectively promote the exfoliation of necrotic tissues and epithelization of wounds. HE and Masson staining suggested that CQDs-NO could promote collagen deposition and epidermal thickness of burn wounds (Fig. 6 ).

Furthermore, immunohistochemical results suggested that CQDs-NO can effectively promote the expression of CD31 positive cells and LAMB1 in deep partial-thickness burn wounds but reduce the number of CD68 positive cells, indicating that CQDs-NO can promote the vascularization of burn wounds and regulate the inflammatory reaction of burn wounds (Fig. 7 ).

Epithelialization is the key step in wound repair. Previous studies suggested that local inflammatory reaction, matrix deposition, and vascularization of the wound could affect the speed of epithelization (Nowell et al., 2016; Chouhan et al., 2017; Frescaline et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2019). The moderate inflammatory reaction is pivotal to immune cells, vascular endothelial cells, and fibroblasts to exert their biological functions after nesting to wounds, thus promoting wound healing. Conversely, the excessive or insufficient inflammatory reaction may cause abnormal proliferation, migration, and secretory function of these cells, thus significantly affecting, i.e., delaying wound healing. For deep partial-thickness burn wounds, which involve the reticular layer of the dermis, the epithelization mainly occurs at a very slow pace, relying on the residual epidermal cells of hair follicles. The existence of necrotic tissue in the wound tends to hinder the vascularization of the wound and the deposition of the wound matrix, leading to slow epithelization and eventually inducing inflammation. During this process, macrophages can gather in the local part of the wound and induce an excessive inflammatory reaction, eventually leading to the prolonged healing time of the deep partial-thickness burn wound (Hoogewerf et al., 2020).

Nitric oxide (NO) is an endogenous gas transmitter, which has an important role in wound healing. NO can cause vasodilation and blood flow increase, affect vascular remodeling (Lundberg et al., 2015; Calabrese et al., 2007), transmit neural information (Garthwaite, 2008) and even kill pathogens (Akaike and Maeda, 2000). Different concentrations of NO can promote endothelial cell proliferation, mediate angiogenesis, and accelerate wound healing, but may also lead to tumor progression or induce inflammation (Lundberg et al., 2015; Calabrese et al., 2007). Since NO has multiple effects and spreads rapidly, it must be applied at a proper dose at a specific site. A low NO concentration can promote cell proliferation, vascularization, and collagen deposition during wound healing (Malone-Povolny et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2019). Meanwhile, a low concentration of NO can inhibit the excessive inflammatory reaction of the wound (Zhang et al., 2019). Thus, a local NO supplement is beneficial to wound healing (Yang et al., 2019; Malone-Povolny et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2019).

NO is unstable in the atmospheric environment, so it was difficult for NO to directly act on the wound with an accurate dose. In this study, we synthesized new CQDs-NO using a two-step hyperthermia-intermittent ultrasonic method. Its raw material is spermidine trihydrochloride, ubiquitous in mammals and easy to chemically synthesize. Therefore, it has a wide range of sources. The preparation method of CQDs-NO needs simple conditions and no other additives except pure water, so the preparation method is stable. CQDs-NO has a particle size of less than 10 nm and high zeta potential, thus being able to form stable suspension liquid in deionized water for at least 1 week. Furthermore, it has photoluminescence properties under the excitation of ultraviolet light, which makes it suitable to be used as an imaging tracer.

FT-IR and MS suggested that there might be groups with the structure of C-N=O on the surface of CQDs-NO. The in vitro experiment showed that CQDs-NO co-cultured with HUVEC cells could release NO continuously, which might be due to the catalysis of multiple enzymes, including glutathione peroxidase in cells. Therefore, CQDs-NO could be used as a NO donor to promote the expression of cell proliferation, migration, and cell adhesion-related proteins. These key proteins are significantly related to wound vascularization and matrix deposition. The results show that CQDs-NO promotes cell migration and protein expression of TGF-β2, HBEGF, and LAMB1, while NO antagonist Carboxy-PITO could inhibit these functions of CQDs-NO, which suggests that CQDs-NO can achieve the above functions by releasing NO.

Transcriptional sequencing confirmed that CQDs-NO could promote the biological processes of vascular endothelial cells, such as angiogenesis, cell-substrate adhesion, extracellular matrix organization, cell migration, and wound healing, which is closely related to wound healing. Among them, we found that CQDs-NO can promote the gene expression of TGFβ2, HBEGF, LAMB1, and other related genes in cells. The gene encoding protein, TGFβ2,is the main gene involved in the aforementioned biological process, which can promote the local chemotaxis of cells to wounds and the synthesis of extracellular matrix (Li et al., 2015; Chen et al., 2017). HBEGF, as a member of the EGF superfamily, can effectively promote cell proliferation and migration (Li et al., 2015; Li et al., 2019; Qiang et al., 2021). In addition, LAMB1 can promote cell adhesion in local wounds (Arimori et al., 2021; Salesa et al., 2021). These cytokines or proteins encoded by these genes have an important role in the process of wound healing. Therefore, CQDs-NO may promote the healing of burn wounds by promoting the synthesis and/or secretion of these cytokines and proteins.

CQDs-NO is a nano material used as a carrier of NO in this study, it is significantly different from other treatments such as growth factors, dressing and artificial dermis commonly used in wound treatment and scientific research. Just like similar reported articles, we set up control group (ddH2O) and treatment groups (400 μg/mL CQDs-NO) (Bankoti et al., 2017).We applied CQDs-NO to deep partial-thickness burn wounds in rats and found that CQDs-O could significantly accelerate the epithelization of wounds compared with the control group. The dose and times of CQDs-NO administration were set by the results of NO release test. Meanwhile, HE staining and Masson staining confirmed that CQDs -NO could effectively promote wound epithelization and collagen deposition as the main component of the wound matrix. Furthermore, immunohistochemical staining showed that CQDs-NO could promote the vascularization of wounds, shown as the number of CD31-positive neovascularization increased significantly. Meanwhile, CQDs-NO promoted the adhesion of cells in the wound, shown as the expression of LAMB1 increased. Besides, immunohistochemical staining also showed that CQDs-NO could regulate the aggregation of inflammatory cells in the wound, shown as the expression of CD68 - positive macrophages decreased significantly. Therefore, this type of CQDs may accelerate the epithelization of deep partial-thickness burn wounds by promoting vascularization of wounds, matrix deposition in the wound bed and inhibiting excessive inflammatory reactions of wounds, and ultimately accelerating wound healing and improving wound healing quality.

The CQDs-NO prepared by the two-step hyperthermia-intermittent ultrasonic method can be used as NO donor. This new material can promote the expression of cytokines and proteins such as TGFβ2, HBEGF, and LAMB1, promote cell proliferation, migration, angiogenesis, epithelialization, and inhibit the excessive inflammatory response reaction of the wound, thus promoting the wound healing.
The authors have nothing to disclose.

Tao Tang: Validation, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Yingying Liu: Validation, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Peng Wang: Validation, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Yang Xiang: Formal analysis, Resources. Lei Liu: Formal analysis, Resources. Shichu Xiao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Project administration. Guangyi Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Project administration.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded by National Natural Science Foundation of China (81671911, 81501645).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.ejps.2023.106394.
